title: Joel
author: Joel, filho de Petuel
body:
Context: Joel prophesied to Judah at an uncertain date (possibly 9th or 5th century BC). The book begins with an immediate calamity — a devastating locust invasion that destroyed the harvests — and uses it as a launching point for announcing the "Day of the Lord," the central theme of biblical prophetic eschatology.

Judgment and appeal: Chapters 1–2 describe the devastation in language that oscillates between the literal and the apocalyptic. The locust army becomes an image of eschatological judgment. The call to repentance is intense: "rend your hearts and not your garments, and return to the Lord your God" (2:13) — internal repentance, not merely external.

The promise of the Spirit (Joel 2:28–32): This is the book's most famous text and its most decisive contribution to biblical theology. "And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions." This text is quoted by Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:17–21) as immediate fulfillment. Joel is the prophet of the Spirit poured out equally on all — men and women, young and old — breaking barriers of gender, generation, and class.
verse: "And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy." <cite>Joel 2:28 — ESV</cite>
